data.content:
Before I build anything out, I need to push back on the brief. Because right now it has three things in it that are pulling in different directions, and if we don't resolve the tension, the campaign will be mediocre.

---

## The problem with "warm, trustworthy, and viral"

These aren't naturally the same thing. Let me show you why.

**What the data actually tells us about Gen Z and health insurance:**

Eight in 10 Gen Z Americans report that medical expenses put at least moderate stress on their budgets. [[1]](https://www.pymnts.com/study_posts/healthcare-on-hold-why-1-in-4-gen-z-consumers-skip-the-doctor/) Over 50% feel anxious or overwhelmed by the mere thought of dealing with insurance â€” and only about 1 in 4 can correctly define basic terms like "deductible" or "co-pay." [[2]](https://medium.com/insurance-2030/is-insurance-ready-for-gen-z-8d21c4be1f57) 68% of Gen Z *with* insurance said their medical debt resulted from their plan not covering the service they received. They want to invest in their health â€” but they need clear pricing transparency to do it. [[3]](https://www.genzhealth.com/)

That's not a warm and fuzzy audience. That's an angry, confused, financially stressed audience who already feels burned by the system.

**What they actually do on TikTok:**

56% of Gen Z go to TikTok for health and wellness advice, and for 1 in 3, it's their main source of health knowledge. [[4]](https://www.healthline.com/health-news/tiktok-main-source-health-information-gen-z) But here's the catch â€” Gen Z says they trust content from qualified registered dietitians more than from unqualified influencers. [[5]](https://nourishingchange.com/when-nutrition-goes-viral/) They are more discerning than the platform's reputation suggests. 66% said they only trusted TikTok wellness advice if it came from a doctor, nutritionist, or similar professional background. [[6]](https://www.zing.coach/fitness-library/healthy-or-not-attitudes-to-wellness-on-tiktok?scLang=en-US)

**And on "viral":**

TikTok's bite-sized viral moments reflect what Gen Z cares about: authenticity, humor, identity, and a break from traditional advertising. [[7]](https://www.contentgrip.com/tiktok-trends-gen-z-marketing-guide/) "Warm and trustworthy" corporate tone is not that. Warm and trustworthy *corporate* content tends to die quietly on TikTok. What spreads is either raw honesty, useful information, or something genuinely surprising.

---

## So here's my challenge back to you

**Three questions before we touch executional ideas:**

**1. What does "viral" mean to you here?**
Reach? Engagement? Actual sign-ups? Because a video that gets 2M views from people who don't qualify for your plan is noise, not success. 44% of Gen Z prefer learning about insurance through relatable, real-life examples [[8]](https://www.securian.com/about-us/newsroom/news-releases/securian-financial-study-reveals-how-gen-z-and-younger-millennials-view-life-and-supplemental-health-insurance.html) â€” that's a content strategy, not a viral strategy. Those are different briefs.

**2. What's the actual enemy?**
The strongest TikTok campaigns for financial/insurance brands don't make the product feel cool â€” they make the *status quo* feel unacceptable. Gen Z perceives insurance as opaque and inconvenient â€” lengthy paperwork, jargon-filled contracts, and sales-driven agents alienate this digital-native generation. [[2]](https://medium.com/insurance-2030/is-insurance-ready-for-gen-z-8d21c4be1f57) That's your villain. Not your competitor. The *category itself* is the villain. Are you brave enough to say that?

**3. What can you actually prove?**
Gen Z employees prioritize mental health, work-life balance, and self-care [[9]](https://news.blueshieldca.com/2025/02/27/what-gen-z-and-millennials-value-in-a-health-plan) above almost everything else in a health plan. More than two in five Gen Zers are in therapy, and claims for behavioral health services spiked 45% between 2023 and 2024. [[1]](https://www.pymnts.com/study_posts/healthcare-on-hold-why-1-in-4-gen-z-consumers-skip-the-doctor/) If your plan has genuinely good mental health coverage, *that* is the campaign. If it doesn't, no amount of warm TikTok content fixes a product problem.

---

## What I'd actually build â€” if the answers check out

**Campaign concept: "Decoded"**

> *Insurance is designed to confuse you. We're going to stop doing that.*

A creator-led series where real people (not actors) go through their actual EOBs, denial letters, and coverage documents â€” and someone from your team explains in plain English what it means and what they're actually entitled to. No script. No polish. Genuine confusion, genuine clarity.

This works because:
- It's useful, which is what earns trust on TikTok â€” not warmth
- It weaponises the category's weakness (opacity) as your strength (transparency)
- The 66% of Gen Z who only trust credentialed voices [[6]](https://www.zing.coach/fitness-library/healthy-or-not-attitudes-to-wellness-on-tiktok?scLang=en-US) will respond to real expertise over influencer performance
- It's naturally shareable â€” people send "this is exactly what happened to me" content

The tone is warm *because it's honest*, not because it's been styled to feel warm. That's the difference.

---

**What I need from you before we go further:**

- What's your actual product strength? Mental health coverage? Price? Simplicity?
- Are we targeting people choosing their first plan, or people already burned by a bad one?
- What does success look like in 90 days â€” reach, leads, or brand lift?

Answer those and I'll build you something worth making.
